front: Define a linear equation in n variables
back: A linear equation in n variables is of the form a₁x₁ + a₂x₂ + ... + aₙxₙ = b, where a₁, a₂, ..., aₙ are coefficients (constants) and b is the constant term.

front: What is a system of linear equations?
back: A system of linear equations is a collection of two or more linear equations involving the same set of variables (unknowns).

front: Define a solution set of a system of linear equations
back: The solution set is the set of all ordered n-tuples (x₁, x₂, ..., xₙ) that satisfy every equation in the system simultaneously.

front: What is a consistent system of equations?
back: A consistent system has at least one solution. It can have exactly one solution (independent) or infinitely many solutions (dependent).

front: What is an inconsistent system of equations?
back: An inconsistent system has no solution. This occurs when the equations represent parallel lines (in 2D) or parallel hyperplanes (in higher dimensions).

front: Define a homogeneous system of linear equations
back: A homogeneous system has all constant terms equal to zero (b = 0 for all equations). It always has at least the trivial solution (all variables = 0).
An augmented matrix combines the coefficient matrix with the column of constants from the system of equations, separated by a vertical line.

front: List the three elementary row operations
back: 1) Swap two rows, 2) Multiply a row by a non-zero scalar, 3) Add/subtract a multiple of one row to another row.

front: What is the Row Echelon Form (REF) of a matrix?
back: A matrix is in REF if: all nonzero rows are above zero rows, leading entry of each nonzero row is to the right of the row above it, and leading entries are the only non-zero entries in their column.

front: What is Reduced Row Echelon Form (RREF)?
back: RREF has all properties of REF plus: leading entry in each nonzero row is 1, and each leading 1 is the only non-zero entry in its column.

front: What is a pivot position in a matrix?
back: A pivot position is the location of a leading 1 (first non-zero entry) in a row when the matrix is in echelon form.
Gaussian elimination is a method to solve systems of linear equations by transforming the augmented matrix to row echelon form using elementary row operations, then using back-substitution.
Gauss-Jordan elimination extends Gaussian elimination to transform the matrix to reduced row echelon form, directly giving the solution without back-substitution.

front: What is the difference between dependent and independent equations?
back: Independent equations contain unique information and none can be derived from others. Dependent equations can be expressed as linear combinations of other equations in the system.

front: When does a homogeneous system have non-trivial solutions?
back: A homogeneous system has non-trivial solutions (infinite solutions other than all zeros) when there are more variables than equations (underdetermined system).
